---
title: "Understanding Word's document format"
format: html
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Understanding Word's document format}
  %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

This document explains how Microsoft Word stores documents internally, which helps understand why certain round-trip challenges exist and how docstyle addresses them. No technical knowledge is assumed.

## What is a DOCX file?

A `.docx` file is actually a ZIP archive containing XML files. You can verify this yourself:

```bash
# Rename and unzip to see the contents
cp document.docx document.zip
unzip document.zip -d document_contents/
```

Inside you'll find:

```
document_contents/
├── [Content_Types].xml     # File type registry
├── _rels/                  # Relationship files
├── word/
│   ├── document.xml        # Main content
│   ├── styles.xml          # Style definitions
│   ├── comments.xml        # Comment content
│   ├── footnotes.xml       # Footnote content
│   └── ...
└── docProps/               # Document properties
```

The key insight: **Word documents are structured data, not opaque binaries.** This is what makes round-tripping possible.

## How Word stores text

Consider this simple sentence in Word:

> The quick **brown** fox.

Word represents this as:

```xml
<w:p>                              <!-- paragraph -->
  <w:r>                            <!-- run (text chunk) -->
    <w:t>The quick </w:t>          <!-- text -->
  </w:r>
  <w:r>
    <w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr>          <!-- run properties: bold -->
    <w:t>brown</w:t>
  </w:r>
  <w:r>
    <w:t> fox.</w:t>
  </w:r>
</w:p>
```

Key concepts:

- **Paragraph (`w:p`)**: A block of text ending with a paragraph mark
- **Run (`w:r`)**: A contiguous chunk of text with the same formatting
- **Text (`w:t`)**: The actual characters
- **Run properties (`w:rPr`)**: Formatting like bold, italic, font

Every time formatting changes, Word starts a new run. This is why "The quick **brown** fox" becomes three runs, not one.

## How Word stores comments

Comments in Word use a "stand-off" markup model. The comment content lives separately from the document text, connected by markers:

```xml
<!-- In document.xml -->
<w:p>
  <w:r><w:t>Review this </w:t></w:r>
  <w:commentRangeStart w:id="1"/>        <!-- Comment starts here -->
  <w:r><w:t>important phrase</w:t></w:r>
  <w:commentRangeEnd w:id="1"/>          <!-- Comment ends here -->
  <w:r>
    <w:commentReference w:id="1"/>       <!-- Clickable marker -->
  </w:r>
  <w:r><w:t> carefully.</w:t></w:r>
</w:p>

<!-- In comments.xml -->
<w:comment w:id="1" w:author="Jane Smith" w:date="2025-01-15T10:30:00Z">
  <w:p>
    <w:r><w:t>This needs clarification</w:t></w:r>
  </w:p>
</w:comment>
```

This design allows comments to:

- Span multiple paragraphs
- Overlap with other comments
- Contain their own formatted text

**The challenge for markdown:** Pandoc's document model is hierarchical (nested elements), but Word's comment markers can cross hierarchy boundaries. A comment might start in one paragraph and end in another—something markdown spans cannot represent.

## How Word stores track changes

Track changes (insertions and deletions) work similarly:

```xml
<w:p>
  <w:r><w:t>The document </w:t></w:r>
  <w:ins w:author="Editor" w:date="2025-01-20T14:00:00Z">
    <w:r><w:t>now </w:t></w:r>
  </w:ins>
  <w:r><w:t>includes </w:t></w:r>
  <w:del w:author="Editor" w:date="2025-01-20T14:01:00Z">
    <w:r>
      <w:delText>old text</w:delText>
    </w:r>
  </w:del>
  <w:r><w:t>new content.</w:t></w:r>
</w:p>
```

- **`w:ins`**: Marks inserted text (green underline in Word)
- **`w:del`**: Marks deleted text (red strikethrough in Word)
- **`w:delText`**: Special element for deleted characters

Both carry author and timestamp metadata.

## The overlapping hierarchy problem

Consider a comment that starts before a tracked insertion and ends after it:

```
Text: "Review [this important] phrase"
Comment spans: "this important phrase"
Insertion: "important "
```

In Word's XML:

```xml
<w:commentRangeStart w:id="1"/>
<w:r><w:t>this </w:t></w:r>
<w:ins>
  <w:r><w:t>important </w:t></w:r>
</w:ins>
<w:r><w:t>phrase</w:t></w:r>
<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="1"/>
```

The comment range *crosses* the insertion boundary. In Pandoc's tree model, this would require:

```
Comment [
  Text "this "
  Insertion [
    Text "important "  ← Can't split here!
  ]
  Text "phrase"
]
```

But `Insertion` is already a container—you can't have Comment both wrap *around* it and end *inside* it without violating tree structure.

## How docstyle solves this

docstyle uses **HTML comment markers** that exist outside Pandoc's content model:

```markdown
`<!-- comment:start id="1" -->`{=html}this [important ]{.ins id="rev_1"}phrase`<!-- comment:end id="1" -->`{=html}
```

The `{=html}` attribute tells Pandoc to treat these as raw HTML, not content. During rendering, docstyle's Lua filter converts them to the appropriate OpenXML markers.

This approach:

1. **Survives Pandoc parsing**: Raw HTML passes through unchanged
2. **Spans any structure**: Comments can wrap tracked changes, multiple paragraphs, anything
3. **Handles orphans gracefully**: Missing end markers become point comments

## What docstyle does with this knowledge

Understanding Word's structure informs docstyle's design:

| Word Concept | docstyle Approach |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Styles in `styles.xml` | Generated from CSS during pre-render |
| Comments in `comments.xml` | Stored in `comments.json`, injected post-render |
| Track changes metadata | Stored in `revisions.json`, passed via Pandoc metadata |
| Field codes (Zotero) | Stored in `field-codes.json`, injected post-render |
| Overlapping ranges | HTML markers + Lua filter conversion |

The sidecar JSON approach mirrors Word's own separation of content and metadata.

## Why round-tripping is hard

Several factors make Word ↔ Quarto conversion challenging:

1. **Loss of structure**: Markdown doesn't distinguish between "Normal" and "Body Text" styles—they both become paragraphs
2. **Overlapping annotations**: As shown above, ranges can cross hierarchical boundaries
3. **Proprietary extensions**: Zotero field codes, custom XML parts, and other extensions store data in ways markdown cannot represent
4. **Formatting granularity**: Word tracks every font change; markdown abstracts away inline formatting details

docstyle addresses these by:

- Using sidecar files to preserve metadata
- Converting spans to stand-off markers where needed
- Injecting Word-specific structures post-render

## Practical implications

When working with docstyle:

1. **Comments spanning complex structures**: If a comment spans tracked changes or multiple paragraphs, docstyle uses HTML markers automatically during import
2. **Orphan markers**: If you accidentally delete one end of a comment marker, docstyle converts it to a point comment (no range, just a marker at the position)
3. **Editing in QMD**: You can accept/reject changes by simply removing the span wrappers—the underlying text remains
4. **Rendering**: `quarto render` + post-processing injects all the Word-specific XML

## Further reading

- [Architecture](architecture.html): Technical details of the processing pipeline
- [Comments and revisions](comments-revisions.html): Workflow for handling comments
- [YAML schema](reference-yaml-schema.html): Configuration reference
- [ECMA-376 Standard](https://www.ecma-international.org/publications-and-standards/standards/ecma-376/): The official OOXML specification (technical)
